a. *When the state courts have jurisdiction.* Foreign governments may sue and be sued in the courts of a state in their federative name (Republic of Mexico v. Arrangois, 11 How. 1; 2 Abb. 437; 3 id. 470; Manning v. State of Nicaragua, 14 How. 517; Delafield v. State of Illinois, 26 Wend. 192; Burrall v. Jewett, 2 Paige, 134; Gibson v. Woodworth, 8 id. 132).

b. Although a state of the United States has adopted an ordinance of secession, it may, nevertheless, sue in the courts of this state (U. S. v. Victor, 16 Abb. 153).

c. The United States or a state may consent to be sued in a state court (The People of Michigan v. Phoenix Bank, 4 Bosw. 382).

d. Of an action on a contract made in a foreign country, not stamped as required by the law of the country where made (Skinner v. Tinker, 34 Barb. 333).

e. To discharge, on *habeas corpus*, persons enlisted in the United States army (Re Carlton, 7 Cow. 471; see Re Dabb, 12 Abb. 113; Re Phelan, 9 id. 286; U. S. v. Wingall, 5 Hill, 16; Re Furguson, 9 Johns. 239; Re Husted, 1 Johns. Cas. 136; see Re Hopson, 40 Barb. 34, contra).

f. Of actions on bonds given for duties to the United States (U. S. v. Dodge, 14 Johns. 95).

g. Of actions by collectors of United States customs, upon receiptor's agreement for safe-keeping of forfeited goods (Sailly v. Cleveland, 10 Wend. 156.)

h. *When the state courts do not have jurisdiction.* They can not entertain an action brought against the state itself, unless authorized to do so by statute (Keirstead v. The People, 1 Abb. 385; Garr v. Bright, 1 Barb. Ch. 157).

i. Nor of an action in which a foreign consul is defendant, either alone or with others (Rock River Bank v. Hoffman, 14 Abb. 72); and it is no answer to an application to vacate the proceedings, in such an action, that, since the action was commenced, the exequator of the said consul has been revoked (id.); nor, in the case of a consul sued jointly with others, can the state court retain jurisdiction of the action by striking out the name of the consul, and proceeding against the other defendants (id.); and see Taaks v. Schmidt, 19 How. 413; Valarino v. Thompson, 3 Selden, 576. The exemption of a consul is not personal, and he can not waive it (Valarino v. Thompson, 3 Selden, 576; and see 1 Barb. 449; 1 Sand. 690).

j. Nor of a trespass on real property in another state (Hurd v. Miller, 2 Hilton, 540; Watts v. Kenney, 23 Wend. 484).

k. Nor to restrain the prosecution of an action in another state (Williams v. Ayrault, 31 Barb. 364; Mitchell v. Bunch, 2 Paige, 606; Meade v. Merritt, id. 402; Baily v. Rider, 10 N. ## SECTION 151.
### MITIGATION.

**a. What admissible evidence.** It is well settled that matter which would sustain a plea of justification is inadmissible under a plea of not guilty (Litt. Sel. Cases, 150; 1 B. Mon. 170). But matter which falls short of a justification may be admitted in mitigation of damages. Hart v. Reed, 1 B. Mon. 170; Thurman v. Virgin and wife, 18 B. Mon. 791.

**b. Words charged must be substantially proved.** The rule of evidence in actions of slander formerly was that the plaintiff must prove the precise words. That rule has been no further relaxed than to admit proof of the substance of the words. It is not enough to prove words of the same effect or import, or conveying the same idea. The words must be substantially the same words, and it is not sufficient that they contain substantially the same *charge*, but in different phraseology; equivalent words of slander will not do. Taylor v. Moran, 4 Met. 127.

**c. Court to determine whether words admissible.** While the proof of speaking the words is for the jury, the correspondence between the words spoken and laid is for the court. *Ib.*

**d. Words spoken after action brought.** In an action of slander, words spoken pending the action, and set up in an amended petition, were admitted in evidence without objection or any admonition to the jury as to the weight or effect they were to give to them; the court instructed the jury that the plaintiff had a right to recover, as well for slanderous words spoken after as before the action was brought, and that in determining the amount of damages they were to consider *all the facts and circumstances proven in the cause*; the case was submitted to the jury and argued by counsel, after which the court said to the jury, in another instruction, that the plaintiff could not recover in this action for words uttered since the filing of the original petition, but that evidence thereof was admissible on the question of malice, and that they could not regard the words as *substantive slanders*, for which they might give damages in the action. *Held*, that the error was not cured by this caution. It should have been full and explicit as to the purpose for which alone the evidence was admissible, embracing specifically the idea that such evidence could not be considered by them to increase the damages. *Ib.*

[Sec. 125, Ohio code, is substantially the same as 151, Kentucky code.]

**e.** In a suit for defamation, evidence of actionable words, or distinct libels, not declared on, is competent to show the *quo animo*; but they can not be made the foundation for a recovery of damages for any injury done by *such words*, or libels; and so far as they can affect the damages, it is solely by showing the *degree of malice* in the publication of the words or libel declared on. Van Derveer v. Sutphin, 5 O. St. 293.

**f.** Matters not capable of supporting an action or constituting a justification, may be given in aggravation or mitigation of damages, as there is no other mode of bringing it before the jury. Fisher v. Patterson, 14 O. R. 418.

**g.** The general character of the plaintiff is in issue in an action of libel, and general reputation of particular acts is not admissible. *Ib.*

a. *Judgment in favor of two plaintiffs one only being entitled.* A petition in the names or payee of a note, and another to whom it passed by delivery, as co-plaintiffs, distinctly stated that the note was the property of the latter, and prayed judgment in his favor alone. **SEC. 405.** *Terms of sale of mortgaged property.* Sales of personal property or slaves, made by order of court, shall be on a credit of three months; sales of real property, on a credit of not less than three nor more than twelve months, or on installments equivalent to not more than twelve months' credit on the whole, to be determined by the court.¹ In all sales on credit, the purchaser shall execute bond, with good surety, to be approved by the person making the sale, which bond shall have the force of a judgment; and in sales of real property, a lien shall be retained on the property for its price. (*Notes to 404.*)

**SEC. 406.** *Personal judgment may be had in actions on mortgages.* In an action on a mortgage or lien, the judgment may be rendered for the sale of the property, and for the recovery of the debt against the defendant personally. (*Notes.*)

**SEC. 407.** *Judgments for money may be set off, and how.* Judgments for the recovery of money may be set off against each other, having due regard to the legal and equitable rights of all persons interested in both judgments. The setoff may be ordered upon motion, after reasonable notice to the adverse party, where both judgments are in the same court, or in an action by equitable proceedings in the court in which the judgment sought to be annulled by the setoff, was rendered.

**SEC. 408.** *Injunction may be obtained before judgment, to prevent loss.* During the pendency of an action, the judgment in which, when recovered, could be used as a setoff against a judgment in favor of the defendants, or either of them, the court, to prevent loss by insolvency, non-residence, or otherwise, may enjoin the collection of the judgment in favor of such defendants, according to the provisions of chapter four, of title eight.²

## SECTION 28.
### JURISDICTION OF COUNTY COURTS.

a. *Bastardy proceedings are civil proceedings.* It would seem that proceedings in cases of bastardy are regarded as civil proceedings, by being placed among that class in the civil code, sec. 28, subdivision 4. (Crenshaw, J.: Conden v. Commonwealth, Ms. Opin., December, 1855.) Chandler v. Commonwealth, 4 Met. 66.

b. *When administration granted on estate of non-resident.* To give jurisdiction to a county court to grant administration of the estate of a person dying in another state, it is necessary to show that, at the time of the death of such person, he had some property, or, at least, a debt owing to him, in the county in which the application is made. The simple assertion of a claim will not give such jurisdiction. Simpson, J.: Wickliffe v. Mason, Ms. Opin., October 1857.

c. *Trial in bastardy case—appearance of defendant not necessary.* Under the Revised Statutes, chap. 6, an appearance by a defendant is not necessary to authorize a trial of a bastardy case. It is not a criminal, but a civil, proceeding. Chandler v. Commonwealth, 4 Met. 66.

d. *Same—how judgment enforced.* Where a defendant in a bastardy case fails to appear, in compliance with his recognizance, the court is not thereby deprived of its jurisdiction over the case, but may give relief, by trial and judgment, against the defendant upon his failure to appear, and, by an attachment, to compel performance of the judgment. *Ib.* 66.

e. *Bastard father liable, though infant.* That the father of a bastard child is an infant, does not relieve him from prosecution, under the statute, for its support. Infants are liable for their tortious acts. But a guardian *ad litem* should be appointed to defend him. *Ib.* 66.

f. *Bastardy cases are special proceedings—not actions.* Under the provisions of the civil code, a bastardy case is a special proceeding, and not an action. It is not for a penalty or forfeiture, nor is it for the enforcement of a private right, within the meaning of the code. *Ib.* 66.

g. *Evidence of a promise of marriage is not admissible on the trial of a bastardy case.* *Ib.* 66.

h. *Appeals do not lie to circuit court, from judgment of county court—when.* The circuit court has no jurisdiction of an appeal from a judgment of the county court, upon a forfeited recognizance for the appearance of a defendant in a bastardy case. An appeal from such judgment is exclusively cognizable by the court of appeals. Commonwealth v. Taphorn, 3 Met. 71.

## SECTION 153.

### ALLEGATIONS OF VALUES.

c. *Not to be taken as true, though not denied.* The code does not authorize the court to take as true allegations of value, or amount of damages laid in the plaintiff's petition, in ordinary cases, though not denied in the answer, and though there be no answer to the petition. Daniel v. Judy, 14 B. Mon. 394.

d. *Same: value of property converted.* The plaintiff alleged the taking and converting a quantity of coal, the property of plaintiff, of a certain value; defendant failed to answer—*Held*, that proof was necessary to show the value of the coal to authorize a judgment for the plaintiff. *Ibid*, 395.

e. *Specific sum alleged to be due and owing for property converted.* When a specific sum is alleged to be due and owing from the defendant on an account for goods, wares, etc., and this not controverted, the intervention of a jury and proof of a plaintiff's demand are unnecessary. The fact that the defendant appeared to the action does not affect the principle. It is the failure to answer which, under the provisions of the code (secs. 153–409), authorizes the court to render judgment. Francis v. Francis, 18 B. Mon. 60.

f. *Same: for services rendered.* In a suit *ex contractu*, as for medical services, etc., where the account was exhibited and damages claimed for the amount, and there was no answer, no proof was necessary, and it was proper for the court to render judgment for the amount of the account without the intervention of a jury. a. *Party for whose use action is brought, must be plaintiff.* The requisition of the code, that every action shall be brought in the name of the real party in interest, is not a matter of form, but of substance; hence, a person for whose use an action is brought, must be made a party by name, and his name must be in the *style* of the suit in the petition. It is not sufficient that it appears in the body of the petition, as the party for whose use the action is brought. Crenshaw, J. : Wilkes, Sheridan, etc. v. Morehead, Ms. Opin., September, 1856.

b. *Suit upon warehouse bond.* The bond of the proprietor of a tobacco warehouse in Louisville, binding him to collect and pay over all moneys arising from the sales of tobacco, to the owner, where the warehouseman fails so to pay the money, may be proceeded upon in the name of the owner (Civil Code, sec. 30), though the bond is executed to the commonwealth. Lane v. Casey, 1 Met. 411.

c. *Equitable title—legal owner necessary party.* Where the plaintiff has only an equitable right to the note sued on, the legal title not having been transferred to him, the payee is a necessary party to the action. Gill v. Johnson, 1 Met. 449.

d. *But equitable owner must sue.* An action for the recovery of a debt not assignable by statute, must be brought in the name of the equitable owner as plaintiff (Civil Code, sec. 30), and the assignor is a necessary party as plaintiff or defendant (*ib.*, sec. 31). A petition by the assignor as plaintiff, suing, as the caption and body of the petition recite, *for the use of the equitable owner*, is insufficient. The latter does not thereby become a party to the action. Lytle v. Lytle, 2 Met. 127.

e. *The party for whose sole benefit a contract is evidently made*, may sue thereon in his own name, although the engagement be not directly to or with him. Allen v. Thomas, 3 Met. 198.

f. *Partition of slaves—administrator necessary party.* It is, in general, true that a suit for the division of slaves descended can not be maintained until an administrator shall have been appointed and made a party; but cases may occur in which this rule will not be applied. Anderson, etc. v. Smith, 3 Met. 491.

g. *Petition stating action to be for benefit of another, makes him party.* One for whose use and benefit it is stated in the petition the action is brought, is thereby virtually made a party to the action, and may, by an amended petition, assume the attitude of plaintiff. Fenwick v. Phillips, 2 Met. 87.

h. *A person who is prayed to be made a defendant, in a petition*, does not thereby become a party to the action. To make him so, process must issue, and service, either actual or constructive must be had. Smith, etc. v. Gower, 3 Met. 171.

i. *Who is the real party in interest.* A plaintiff who has an absolute right to the money due on a note, and to receive and appropriate it to his own use when recovered, is the real party in interest, although the payee of the note may be interested in the event of the suit, in such wise, that if the note be not collected he will not receive anything as its price, or by reason of his indorsement and sale of it. a. A statement of the partial evidence of a fact is not an averment of the fact. The averment in a pleading that a vessel foundered at sea on the day of leaving the port, without any collision, or any storm, or any visible cause, is not equivalent to an averment that the vessel was unseaworthy when she commenced her voyage, although the facts averred, when proved, might warrant a jury in inferring that the vessel was unseaworthy (Page v. Boyd, 11 How. 417).—In an action for a breach of promise of marriage, the complaint did not directly allege any promise, but alleged that in a conversation between the parties the plaintiff asserted among other things that he, defendant, had promised to marry her, and that defendant acknowledged that he had done wrong in promising her as he did, and hoped she would forgive him, but that if he should marry her as they had talked, and she go to his home, it would make both miserable for life. The defendant demurred on the ground that the complaint did not state facts sufficient to constitute a cause of action. The demurrer was allowed, on the ground that the complaint did not show the making any promise by the defendant (Buzzard v. Knapp, 12 How. 504). It is not sufficient to give such a statement as if submitted to a jury they might infer a fact or facts constituting a cause of action or defense, but the statement should be such an one that the law alone, without the aid of a jury, draws a conclusion of the existence of a cause of action or defense. Page v. Boyd, 11 How. 417.

b. Allegations of duty—what sufficient. An allegation of the duty is of no avail, unless, from the rest of the complaint, the facts necessary to raise the duty can be collected. To aver that, by a certain judgment recovered in the courts of this or any other state, or, by reason of a certain bond executed by the defendant, he was indebted to the plaintiff in a given sum, or owed any other duty (Throop v. Hatch, 3 Abb. 25), would not constitute any fact of which a court would take notice. (And see Boorman v. Brown, 4 Per. & D. 401.) City of Buffalo v. Halloway, 3 Selden, 493.

b. *Offenses included in offense charged.* On an indictment for an offense consisting of different degrees, the defendant may be found guilty of any degree not higher than that charged in the indictment, and may be found guilty of any offense included in the offense charged. Commonwealth v. Garland, 3 Met. 479.

c. *Gaming with negroes.* The first and second sections of the act of 1858, against gaming with negroes (1 Rev. Stat. 572), define an offense consisting of two degrees. To convict of the first degree it is necessary to show that "money or other thing of value" shall have been bet, won, or lost, whatever may have been the amount or the value of the money or thing so bet; and it is not necessary to prove the *value* as alleged. To convict of the lower degree, as defined by the second section, it is only necessary to prove the *gaming*. And, under an indictment charging the facts necessary to constitute the higher degree of the offense, the defendant may be convicted of the lower degree. *Ib.*

f. *Court may order restoration after divorce.* Under sec. 6 of art. 3, chap. 47 of the Revised Statutes, it is not necessary that the decree of divorce should order the restoration of the property obtained by either party from or through the other, before or during the marriage, in consideration or by reason thereof. not disposed of at the commencement of the suit; but such order might be made afterward. So far, however, as the section, *supra*, was designed to regulate the mode of enforcing the right of restoration of property acquired during marriage, it has been superseded by sec. 462 of the civil code. *Williams v. Gooch*, 2 Met. 486.

g. *Controversy concerning property may be settled by subsequent proceedings.* The order of restoration in the final judgment for divorce, contemplated by sec. 462 of the civil code, *supra*, is a formal one, to be made in cases in which no mention of the property to be restored has been made in the pleadings, and was not designed to apply to any specific property, nor to settle any controversy with reference thereto, even between the divorced parties. After the order has been made, any controversy that may arise concerning the property is to be settled by subsequent proceedings. *Ib.* 486.

a. *Redundant matter*. Redundant and irrelevant are not equivalent terms. Matter which is irrelevant, it is true, is also redundant: but the converse is by no means true. A needless repetition of material averments is redundancy; although the facts averred so far from being irrelevant, may constitute the whole cause of action (*Bowman v. Sheldon*, 5 Sand. 660). Allegations of aggravating circumstances in an action for malicious prosecution held not to be redundant (*Brockleman v. Brandt*, 10 Abb. 141). *Held*, *contra* in action for breach of promise of marriage (*Smith v. Waite*, 7 How. 227). Where to a complaint alleging want of probable cause, the answer after denying the allegation of the complaint, set up as a defense that defendant had probable cause; *held*, that the latter defense was redundant. *Rost v. Harris*, 12 Abb. 446; see *Benedict v. Seymour*, 6 How. 303.

b. *Irrelevant*. Matter is *irrelevant* in a pleading which has no bearing on the subject matter of the controversy, and can not affect the decision of the court (*Fabricotti v. Launitz*, 1 Code Rep. N. S. 121; *Stafford v. Mayor of Albany*, 6 Johna. 25; *Van Rensselaer v. Brice*, 4 Paige, 177; *Perrine v. Farr*, 2 Zab. 356; *Lee Bank v. Kitching*, 11 Abb. 435; *Cahill v. Palmer*, 17 Abb. 196). If material only as affecting the damages (*Root v. Foster*, 9 How. 37), or the costs, it is not irrelevant (*Van Rensselaer v. Brice*, 4 Paige, 177). Hence, the true test of the materiality of averments sought to be struck out is, to inquire whether such averments tend to constitute a cause of action or defense, and if they do, they are not irrelevant. *Ingersoll v. Ingersoll*, 1 Code R. 102; *Averill v. Taylor*, 5 How. 476; 1 Code Rep. N. S. 404; *Minor v. Terry*, 6 How. 208.

d. *Grounds of attachment by surety against principal.* That the plaintiff is a surety in a debt without an indemnity is not of itself sufficient ground for an attachment against the principal. Sec. 4 of chap. 97 of the Revised Statutes (page 655) is repealed by the provisions of the Civil Code; and provisional remedies in behalf of a surety against his principal must be obtained upon the grounds and in the mode enumerated in the code (Civil Code, secs. 875, 748, 728, 729, 730). Patterson v. Caldwell, 1 Met. 489.

e. *Who liable to surety.* R. and H. executed their joint note to McM. in part payment for a negro; afterward H. sold his interest in the purchase to R., who agreed to pay the note; McM. sued and obtained judgment against both R. and H.; R., who had property sufficient to satisfy the execution, replevied the debt, with P. as surety; the sheriff who had not seen H., stating to P., before he signed the bond, that H. would also execute it. H. subsequently refused to join in the replevin bond. R. became insolvent, and P. had the debt to pay, and sued H. for the amount. *Held*, that the judgment was merged by the execution of the replevin bond, and H. discharged from all liability upon it; that the payment of the bond was for the benefit of R., for whom alone P. was surety, and that P. can not recover of H. (Kouns v. Bank of Ky., 2 B. Mon. 303). The case in 3 B. Mon. 9, not analagous. Hoskins v. Parsons, 1 Met. 251.

f. *Bastardy cases are special proceedings—not actions.* Under the provisions of the civil code, a bastardy case is a special proceeding, and not an action. It is not for a penalty or forfeiture, nor is it for the enforcement of a private right, within the meaning of the code. *Ib.* 66.

g. *Evidence of a promise of marriage is not admissible on the trial of a bastardy case.* *Ib.* 66.

h. *Appeals do not lie to circuit court, from judgment of county court—when.* The circuit court has no jurisdiction of an appeal from a judgment of the county court, upon a forfeited recognizance for the appearance of a defendant in a bastardy case. An appeal from such judgment is exclusively cognizable by the court of appeals. Commonwealth v. Taphorn, 3 Met. 71.

f. *Same. Notice must be specially averred.* When notice of the acceptance of an offer to guaranty is requisite (in an action on such guaranty), the allegation of notice in the petition should be special, and such as will enable the court to determine from its statements whether or not the notice was given as the law requires (2 Ala. 373; 3 Conn. 438). The general averment, "of all which the defendants had notice," is not sufficient. Steadman v. Guthrie, 4 Met. 149.
